tips.title: Sé wears an accent so it isn't se
tips.body: Sé (I know, from the verb saber) and se (the reflexive pronoun from Day 15) are spelled almost the same. The accent is the only thing separating 'I know' from 'himself/herself.' Watch for it when you're reading.

tips.title: Hay never changes, no matter how many things there are
tips.body: Hay means both 'there is' and 'there are' -- it's always the same word whether one thing exists or a hundred do. 'Hay un chico' and 'hay dos chicos' both use hay, unlike English, which switches between 'is' and 'are.'

tips.title: Creo que is a gentle way to share an opinion
tips.body: Creo (I believe/think) is almost always followed by que, forming 'creo que...' (I think that...). It's one of the most useful sentence-openers in everyday Spanish for softly stating what you think.

bonusCards.english: I know that there is a good friend here.
Do you know if he can go with me?
I believe there is nothing here, but I do not know.
There is a good place, but I do not know where.
There is nobody here; I believe everyone has gone.

resources.name: MundoDELE — Hay in Spanish: There Is, There Are & Haber
